message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine the SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence among school workers in the setting of full in-person schooling and the highly transmissible Omicron variants of concern.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cross-sectional study among school staff, comparing to period-, age-, sex- and postal code-weighted data from Canadian blood donors from the same community.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Three large school districts in the greater Vancouver metropolitan area, British Columbia, Canada, with serology sampling done between January 26, 2022 and April 8, 2022.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>School staff actively working in the Vancouver, Richmond and Delta School Districts.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence based on nucleocapsid (N)-protein testing, adjusted for the sensitivity and specificity of the assay.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A majority (65.8%) of the 1845 school staff enrolled reported close contact with a COVID-19 case outside the household. Of those, about half reported close contact with a COVID-19 case at school either in a student (51.5%) or co-worker (54.9%). In a representative sample of 1620 (87.8%) school staff, the adjusted seroprevalence was 26.5% [95%CrI: 23.9 – 29.3%]. This compared to an age, sex and residency area-weighted seroprevalence of 32.4% [95%CrI: 30.6 – 34.5%] among 7164 blood donors.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Despite frequent COVID-19 exposures, the prevalence of SARS-CoV-2 infections among the staff of three main school districts in the Vancouver metropolitan area was no greater than a reference group of blood donors, even after the emergence of the more transmissible Omicron variant.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What is already known on this subject?</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Earlier studies indicate that COVID-19 infection rates are not increased among school staff at previous stages of the pandemic compared to the community, yet controversy remains whether this will remain true after the emergence of the highly transmissible Omicron variant.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What this study adds?</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Despite frequent COVID-19 exposures, this study identified no detectable increase in SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence among school staff working in three metro Vancouver public school districts after the first Omicron wave in British Columbia, compared to a reference group of blood donors from the same age, sex and community area.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence among public school staff in Metro Vancouver after the first Omicron wave in British Columbia, Canada

reference.unstructured: Skowronski D , Sekirov I , Sabaiduc S , et al. Low SARS-CoV-2 sero-prevalence based on anonymized residual sero-survey before and after first wave measures in British Columbia, Canada, March-May 2020. medRxiv 2020:1–26. doi: https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.07.13.20153148 [published Online First: July 15, 2020]
Canadian Blood Services. COVID-19 seroprevalence brief report. Available from: https://www.covid19immunitytaskforce.ca/results-blood-donation-organizations/. [Accessed July 4, 2022].

COVID-19 Immunity Task Force. Field studies working groups core data elements (individual). Available from: https://www.covid19immunitytaskforce.ca/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/Core-Elements_English-New.pdf [Accessed July 4, 2022].
Choi A , Mâsse LC , Bardwell S , et al. Symptomatic and asymptomatic transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in K-12 schools, British Columbia, April to June 2021. Microbiol Spect [in press]
